summary: I remember a time when the book The Informer was playing around town, and I was down at T's Bookstore. T asked me if I had a first edition of The Informer, and I said no. I knew it was by Liam O'Flaherty, but Targ insisted it was by Sean O'Casey. It was a funny moment because I realized I might have been collecting the wrong author all these years.

There was a mix-up once at T's Bookstore about the authorship of The Informer. Targ was convinced it was by Sean O'Casey, even though I knew it was by Liam O'Flaherty. It was a bit of a joke between us, and I left him to think over his mistake. That moment stuck with me because it showed how people can get authors mixed up.

I recall when The Informer was playing around town, and I was at T's Bookstore trying to sell some first editions. T asked if I had a first edition of The Informer, and we got into a debate about who wrote it—Liam O'Flaherty or Sean O'Casey. It was a memorable moment because it made me realize how much I cared about the authors I collected. The book itself was quite well-known at the time.

I've lived in Chicago all my life, and I know the city well. I remember riding streetcars and spotting farmers by how they rush for empty seats. Chicago had its share of characters, like Indian Joe who ran a speed trap outside the city. During Prohibition, I used to hang out in saloons here, where you could get your beer spiked if the barkeep knew you. Chicago was full of stories, from gangsters to everyday folks.

One weekend during Prohibition, T. and I took a trip up to Windsor, Ontario, and back. We had a bottle of bonded liquor with us from Chicago, intending to drink it on the way. But when B., who was straightlaced and moral, wanted to come along, we ended up carrying that bottle the whole trip without even smelling it. It was a funny experience, sneaking liquor across the border during those times.

T., B., and I once took a trip to Fort Wayne, Indiana. We stayed at a hotel where T. and I shared a room, and B. had his own. We played a prank on B. by putting ice cubes in his bed while he was out, and he ended up spending half the night outside our door. That trip was memorable because of the fun and mischief we got into together.

During Prohibition, the saloons around here only sold near beer, unless the barkeep knew you. If you asked for near beer, he might ask if you wanted it straight or spiked, and if spiked, he'd sneak a few drops of alcohol in. I used to get my beer spiked just to see if the barkeep's hand would slip, but it never did. Prohibition made drinking a secretive and tricky business in Chicago.

I remember the last fight between Joe Louis and Max Schmeling. I was listening to the fight at a gas station on Cottage out south here. Louis knocked Schmeling out almost before it got started. After the fight, a guy came up wanting to hear it, but the fight was already over, and he was disappointed. That fight stuck with me because of how quick and decisive it was.

I recall the last fight between Max Schmeling and Joe Louis. It was a quick knockout, with Louis winning almost before the fight began. I was at a gas station on Cottage listening to the fight, and after it ended, a man came looking to hear the fight but was too late. That fight was a big event around here and left a strong impression on me.

One night at Grant Park, I was behind the critics' row at a concert and noticed an old man stealing glances at a paper. After the concert, I managed to see what it was—it was the Daily Racing Form. It struck me as funny that even a critic at a concert would be so interested in the racing form. That moment stuck with me as a glimpse into the city's varied interests.

Indian Joe used to run a speed trap outside Chicago, but the Automobile Association finally got him. I know a guy named C. who pulled a fast one on Indian Joe by writing a fake check to pay a fine. Indian Joe didn't realize he'd been tricked until he tried to cash the check and the teller laughed at him. The Automobile Association played a role in putting a stop to Indian Joe's racket.

A pal of C.'s, who was indicted for murder, skipped to Wisconsin to lay low. After a while, he came back to Chicago and got picked up for speeding, but wasn't recognized. C., who had been a deputy in the district attorney's office before Prohibition, called the police and arranged to have the man turned over to him. They drove back to Wisconsin, and though the man was caught later, C. got away with it all.
